escaping.definition: getting away from something you don't want
escaping.example: There is no escaping the rain today.

influences.definition: changes or affects how someone thinks or acts

significant.example: This is a significant moment in her life.

experiences.definition: things that happen to you in life
experiences.example: Childhood experiences shape who we become.

patterns.definition: ways of doing things that repeat again and again
patterns.example: She noticed patterns in her own behavior.

relationships.definition: connections between people — family, friends, partners

unconsciously.definition: without knowing you are doing it; automatically
unconsciously.example: She unconsciously copied her mother's habits.

prisoner.definition: someone who is trapped and cannot be free
prisoner.example: You don't have to be a prisoner of old habits.

inherited.definition: received from your parents or family — not just money, also habits and ways of thinking
inherited.example: She inherited her mother's love of cooking.

conscious.definition: aware; knowing what you are doing and why
conscious.example: Make a conscious choice to listen more.

react.definition: to act quickly without thinking, because of a feeling
react.example: Don't react in anger — take a breath first.

respond.definition: to act after thinking about what is best — different from react
respond.example: She chose to respond calmly instead of shouting.

rupture.definition: a break or crack in a relationship — when something goes wrong between two people
rupture.example: A small rupture can be healed with an apology.

repair.definition: to fix something that was broken — here, fixing a relationship after a mistake
repair.example: Saying sorry is the first step of repair.

perfection.definition: being completely perfect with no mistakes — impossible for real people
perfection.example: Don't aim for perfection — aim for good enough.

acknowledge.definition: to say that something happened; to admit it openly
acknowledge.example: He acknowledged his mistake and said sorry.

dismissing.definition: treating something as not important; ignoring someone's feelings
dismissing.example: Dismissing a child's fear makes it worse.

inevitable.definition: sure to happen; cannot be avoided
inevitable.example: Mistakes are inevitable — everyone makes them.

profound.definition: very deep and important; changes how you understand something
profound.example: That conversation had a profound effect on me.

withstand.definition: to be strong enough to survive something difficult
withstand.example: Their friendship could withstand any disagreement.

conflict.definition: a fight or disagreement between people
conflict.example: Some conflict in a family is normal.

responsibility.definition: owning what you did — saying 'this was my action'
responsibility.example: Taking responsibility means not making excuses.

resilience.definition: the ability to recover from hard times; to bounce back
resilience.example: Children build resilience through small challenges.

robust.definition: strong and able to handle difficulty
robust.example: A robust relationship can survive arguments.

worthy.example: Every child is worthy of love and respect.

surge.definition: a sudden strong wave of feeling
surge.example: She felt a surge of anger when he lied.

anxiety.definition: a feeling of worry and fear that something bad will happen

proportionate.definition: matching in size or strength — not too much, not too little
proportionate.example: Was your anger proportionate to what happened?

triggered.definition: set off a strong emotional reaction, usually because of a past experience
triggered.example: The loud noise triggered memories of her childhood.

perceived.definition: how she saw it; what she believed was happening (may not be the full truth)
perceived.example: He perceived the comment as an insult, but it wasn't meant that way.

helplessness.definition: the feeling of not being able to do anything; being stuck
helplessness.example: Helplessness is a scary feeling for a child.

reflected.definition: thought carefully about something that already happened
reflected.example: She reflected on why she had reacted so strongly.

patience.definition: the ability to wait calmly without getting angry
patience.example: Children need adults who have patience.

frustration.definition: the feeling when things don't go the way you want
frustration.example: He felt frustration when he couldn't solve the problem.

scolding.definition: telling someone off angrily for doing something wrong
scolding.example: Scolding a child doesn't teach them — it just makes them afraid.

internally.definition: on the inside; in your mind and feelings, not visible from outside
internally.example: Internally, she felt proud even though she didn't show it.

generations.definition: groups of people born around the same time — grandparents, parents, children
generations.example: This habit has been passed down through generations.

curiosity.definition: wanting to learn and understand more; asking why
curiosity.example: Approach your feelings with curiosity, not judgment.

compassion.definition: caring about someone who is suffering; wanting to help them feel better
compassion.example: Have compassion for the child you once were.
